meta.epoch: Year 0 — The Collapse (Tower of Babel) · ~2200 BCE
meta.description: A chronicle of major events in Telumë from the Collapse to the present age. The Haldorian Reckoning counts from Year 0. Approximate Common Era equivalents are given for reference. Compiled from Sūrya, Haldorian, Ealdor, and Kichigamswak sources. Disputed events are noted. Gaps are preserved.
meta.lastUpdated: Year 3450 — Present Day · ~1250 CE · Little Ice Age onset

eras.name: Before the Collapse
eras.description: The age of one language. Adzmyst spoken by all. The Rabbit walks the earth. Nokomis tends the fire.

events.event: Mategwas carries fire from the underworld. Nokomis names him Fyrsthara — the Fire-Carrier.
events.source: The Lost Tales of Telumë, Tale I

events.event: Mategwas weaves his brother Lox from twigs, grass, fur, and light. Lox squanders the gift of fire and freezes. Raccoon finds him in the spring. He learns nothing.
events.source: The Lost Tales of Telumë, Tale VII
events.disputeNote: The Sūrya priests reject this tale. They claim Lox was not woven but born, and that he froze not from carelessness but from challenging the Sun-King's ancestor.

events.event: The Tower is built on the plain of Shinar. Mategwas speaks the first mantras at its base. The builders do not listen.
events.source: The Lost Tales of Telumë, Tale I · The Dawnstone · The Book of Babel

eras.name: The Collapse
eras.description: The Tower falls. The language shatters into five fragments. The storm god Tarḫunt departs. The Flood reshapes the Bay of Ealdforn. The peoples scatter.

events.event: The Tower falls. The one language shatters. The peoples are scattered. The storm god Tarḫunt departs — where he went is not recorded.
events.source: Nešatta Codex, Tablets 1–2 · The Book of Babel

events.event: Mategwas vanishes at the scattering. He is not seen again — except when he is.
events.source: The Lost Tales of Telumë, Coda
events.disputeNote: The Sūrya priests claim he ascended into the Sun. The Kichigamswak elders say he went north and is still running. The Nešatta archivists file him under 'whereabouts unknown — presumed present.'

events.event: The Flood. Melting ice reshapes the Bay of Ealdforn. Sea routes open. The coastline takes its present form.
events.source: Kichigamswak oral tradition · Prūsisk amber records

events.event: First siege of Aristopol by Ealdor. The Markfolk attempt to take the cliff-city at the river narrows. The siege fails. The walls hold.

eras.name: The Early Kingdoms
eras.description: The four realms take shape. Sūrya builds its temple-complex. Ealdor elects its kings. The Haldorian city-states war and trade. The Dawnstone is carved.

events.event: The Dawnstone is carved at Sūryapura. The Trilingual Codex is begun. Vedic Sanskrit, Avestan, and Old Persian on three faces of a single monolith.

The Ātravan Schism — Burning of the Eastern Archives. The berserker Ātarš-Gaēθa leads a night raid on three Metoullin scriptoriums. Eleven priests killed. Palm-leaf copies of the Agnihotra Śāstra burned. He paints his taunt on the wall: 'Fire is first. The king is ash.'
events.source: Sūrya court records · Ātravan oral tradition · Tihomir chronicle
events.disputeNote: Metoullin sources claim the archives were rebuilt within a year. Ātravan sources claim the fire from the burning shelves spread to three villages. The Tihomir chronicle notes both claims and adds: 'The fire knows what it burned.'

events.event: A young Ātravan berserker — unnamed, the Fire-Place carrier — is separated from his warband during the retreat. He crosses into Ealdor carrying a copper brazier with a coal from the eternal flame. A peasant named Cerdic, son of Wulf, encounters him on the road south of Ealdenburg. King Æthelwulf places the boy in the care of the Galdr-Gemyndigan at the Ring. The coal is still burning.
events.source: Ealdenbóc, The Genealogies · Galdr-Gemyndigan monastery records

events.event: Ātarš-Gaēθa is captured and executed by fire at Sūryapura. The Sun-King himself lights the pyre. The berserker's last word is 'ātarš.' His sword is never recovered. The Ātravan say it was smuggled to Ealdor. The Metoullin say it was melted down. The Tihomir chronicle notes: 'The young berserker at the Ring has still not spoken. The coal is still burning. The fire knows where the sword is.'
events.source: Sūrya court records · Ātravan oral tradition · Tihomir chronicle

events.event: Fourth siege of Aristopol by Ealdor. The city nearly falls. Tsarnigrad sends ships. The siege breaks. The Haldorian city-states realize they must unify or perish.
events.source: Ealdenbóc, The Genealogies · Haldorian Republic charter preamble

events.event: The Pankus Accord is signed at Nešatta. The Haldorian Republic is chartered. Seven city-states. No king. No capital. The assembly meets every seven years. The 'no king' clause is carved above every meeting hall — except in Tsarnigrad, where it will be effaced and recarved multiple times over the centuries.

eras.name: The Long Silence
eras.description: A period of consolidation, slow growth, and few written records. The kingdoms stabilize. Trade routes open. The monastic traditions begin in oral form. The Agnihotra Śāstra is composed and censored. The Ātravan tradition goes underground. The Galdr-Gemyndigan begin their oral chronicles at the Ring. Few documents survive from this era. What remains is fragmentary.

events.event: The Agnihotra Śāstra is composed by the Metoullin priesthood. The Sun-King's censors remove three verses. The Ātravan reject the text in its entirety and compose their own oral tradition — the Ātarš Apstāna.
events.source: Agnihotra Śāstra colophon · Ātravan oral tradition
events.disputeNote: The exact date of composition is unknown. Metoullin sources claim the text was revealed by the fire itself at the founding of the Agnihotra temple. Ātravan sources claim it was written by a committee of priests to curry favor with the throne.

events.event: The Galdr-Gemyndigan order begins its oral chronicle at the Ring. The Ātravan coal brought in Year 279 is still burning. A succession of 'silent brothers' — descendants or successors of the original young berserker — tend it. None of them speak. The Galdr-Gemyndigan learn to read the coal's flickering.
events.source: Galdr-Gemyndigan oral tradition · later transcribed in the Ring Chronicle

events.event: The Nešatta archive reaches its present form. Clay tablets organized by era and language. The glyph archive is sealed in the deepest chamber. Only the chief archivist may enter.

The Trilingual Codex is copied and recopied. The original Dawnstone shows signs of weathering. The Old Persian face is nearly illegible. The Avestan face is worn but readable. The Vedic face is repainted in gold leaf by order of the Sun-King.

eras.name: The Age of Chronicles
eras.description: The great written compilations. The monastic orders are formally chartered. The oral traditions are set down on vellum. The Medieval Warm Period enables travel, trade, and the exchange of manuscripts between the realms.

events.event: The Tihomir Monastery is formally chartered on the Magdalen Islands. Old Church Slavonic monks. Witnesses, not voters in the Pankus. They begin keeping the written record. The monastery's charter specifies that it will never vote, never hold office, and never refuse a request for shelter.

Bogumil transcribes the Nešatta Codex at Tihomir Monastery. Seven tablets. Three translatable. One missing (removed by a delegation from Tsarnigrad). One in an unreadable script. One sealed with clay impressed with a glyph Bogumil refuses to reproduce. One blank — clay fired, surface prepared, nothing written. Bogumil notes: 'I have copied what I cannot read. The gaps are my ignorance, not the stone's.'

The Galdr-Gemyndigan order is formally chartered at the Ring. Their founding document acknowledges 'the silent brother from the south' — the successor of the original young Ātravan, still tending the coal after nearly three thousand years. The coal is still burning. The charter specifies that the order will keep both flames — the Ring fire and the Ātravan coal — burning in perpetuity, but will never allow them to touch.

Brother Eadwine compiles The Lost Tales of Telumë at Tihomir Monastery. He synthesizes Sūrya, Nešatta, and Kichigamswak sources. His colophon notes: 'They do not agree. I have not made them agree. The Rabbit does not want them to agree.'
events.source: The Lost Tales of Telumë, Coda

events.event: The Ealdenbóc (Se Ǣrest Hlāford Hengist) is set down in writing by the Galdr-Gemyndigan. The oral traditions of the Markfolk, Fellfolk, and Tuathafolk are recorded. The genealogies are compiled. The sayings of the wise are collected. Marginal corrections from living skalds are included — some angry, some grieving.

eras.name: The Warm Period Peak
eras.description: The Medieval Warm Period at its height. Sea routes fully open. Trade flourishing. The Pankus meets without interruption. The kingdoms are connected. The great libraries expand.

events.event: The Pankus meets for the 412th time since the Accord. All seven city-states send delegations. The 'no king' clause is reaffirmed. Tsarnigrad abstains.

The Sun-King's library at Sūryapura reaches its greatest extent. Over three thousand palm-leaf manuscripts. The Agnihotra Śāstra is recopied with new commentary. The censored verses are still missing. The Ātravan still remember them.

eras.name: The Present Age · The Cooling
eras.description: The Medieval Warm Period declines. The Little Ice Age begins. Sea routes narrow. The northern passages freeze. The kingdoms face pressure from climate, from each other, and from forces older than the Collapse.

events.event: The first recorded failure of the Pankus to convene. Three city-states send word that the sea routes are impassable. The assembly is postponed. It meets two years late.

Senator Claudius, writing from the Bay of Ealdforn, references the Nešatta Codex in a letter to the Pankus. He quotes the central prohibition: 'LUGAL-uš lē ēšta — a king shall not be.' The letter warns of 'the cold that comes from the north and the hunger that comes from the cold.' The letter is entered into the Republican record.

Tsarnigrad stockpiles grain. The other city-states accuse it of hoarding. Tsarnigrad replies that it is preparing for 'the winter that will not end.' The Pankus debates sanctions. No decision is reached.
events.disputeNote: Tsarnigrad denies hoarding. The Tihomir chronicle notes: 'Tsarnigrad has always prepared for winters. This winter is different. The granaries are full. The gates are closed. The city does not explain itself.'

events.event: The sea ice reaches the northern fjords of Ealdor. Fellfolk ships can no longer reach the open water. The Thing is deadlocked — some Jarls want to raid south, some want to stay and freeze, some want to abandon the old territory entirely.

Present day. The Little Ice Age advances. The Pankus convenes at Nešatta for what may be the last time before the ice makes the journey impossible. The Nešatta Codex is read aloud. The seventh tablet is still blank. The storm god has not returned. The coal at the Ring is still burning.
events.source: Current · This chronicle is being written now

ongoing.name: The Ātravan Coal
ongoing.description: The Ātravan coal at the Ring monastery. Brought by the young berserker in Year 279. Still burning as of the latest record (Year 3450). The Galdr-Gemyndigan tend it alongside their own fire. The two flames have never been allowed to touch. The succession of silent brothers — descendants or successors of the original young berserker — has continued unbroken for over three thousand years. The current silent brother is the eighty-seventh of the line. He has never spoken. The coal is still burning.

ongoing.name: The Sword of Ātarš-Gaēθa
ongoing.description: Never recovered after his execution in Year 281. The Ātravan claim it was smuggled to Ealdor and hidden at the Ring. The Metoullin claim it was melted down and reforged. The Tihomir chronicle has recorded seventeen separate claims of its discovery over the centuries. None have been verified. The Ātravan say the sword will ignite only for the true heir of Ātarš-Gaēθa — and that heir has not yet appeared.
ongoing.lastRecorded: Year 3400 — Senator Claudius notes: 'A mercenary in Aristopol claims to carry the burning sword. He charges money to show it. The sword does not burn. The mercenary is a fraud. But there is a scar on his palm that looks like an old burn.'

ongoing.name: The Seventh Tablet
ongoing.description: The seventh tablet of the Nešatta Codex. Blank. Clay fired. Surface prepared. Nothing written. The storm god Tarḫunt has not returned to write the final tablet. The Pankus waits. The tablet is displayed at every assembly. Delegates are invited to write on it. No one has. Bogumil noted: 'I do not know what this means. The abbot says it means the covenant is not finished.'
ongoing.lastRecorded: Year 3450 — Present day. The tablet is still blank.

disputed.description: What happened to Mategwas at the Scattering? Sūrya: ascended into the Sun. Kichigamswak: went north, still running. Nešatta: whereabouts unknown — presumed present.

Was Lox woven by Mategwas (Kichigamswak telling) or born as his twin (Sūrya telling)? The Sūrya priests reject the weaving story as 'folk superstition.' The Kichigamswak elders reject the birth story as 'priestly arrogance.'
Where is the sword of Ātarš-Gaēθa? Ring monastery? Sūryapura (melted down)? Eastern mountains? The seventeen claims of discovery are documented in the Tihomir chronicle. None verified.

Is the coal at the Ring truly the original coal brought by the young berserker in Year 279? The Galdr-Gemyndigan insist it is. Skeptics note that coal cannot burn for three thousand years. The Galdr-Gemyndigan reply that the coal is not burning — the fire is. The coal is just what the fire lives in.

Are the silent brothers truly a continuous lineage from the original young berserker? The Galdr-Gemyndigan maintain a genealogy. Critics note gaps in the early centuries. The current silent brother does not speak. He cannot be asked.
